challenges.prompt: Put the stages of a transformer in the correct order, from raw text at the bottom to the predicted next token at the top.
lines: Look up each token to get its embedding vector
Add positional information so word order is preserved
Pass the sequence up through the stack of identical blocks
The final layer produces a score for every token in the vocabulary
Pick the next token from those scores
challenges.explanation: The pipeline is: text -> tokens -> embeddings -> add position -> stack of transformer blocks -> final scores (logits) over the vocabulary -> choose the next token. The middle stage (the stack of identical blocks, each doing attention then feed-forward) is the transformer itself; everything before prepares the input and everything after reads out a prediction.

challenges.prompt: Complete the pseudocode for one transformer block. The two main ingredients are self-attention and the feed-forward network, and each is wrapped in a residual connection (add its result back onto x).
challenges.codeTemplate:
def transformer_block(x):
    # tokens communicate and gather context
    x = x + {{blank}}(layer_norm(x))
    # each token is refined on its own
    x = x + {{blank}}(layer_norm(x))
    return x
challenges.explanation: Every block runs two sub-layers in sequence: first self-attention (the communication step, where tokens look at each other), then a feed-forward network / MLP (the computation step, where each token is transformed on its own). The `x = x + ...` around each is the residual connection, which lets information flow straight up the stack while each sub-layer only learns a small adjustment.

challenges.prompt:
A transformer receives its input but, due to a bug, positional information is NEVER added to the embeddings. The model is then asked to distinguish these two sentences:

A: "dog bites man"
B: "man bites dog"

What happens?
challenges.code:
# positional information step was skipped!
embeddings = lookup(tokens)   # just the token vectors
# (no  + positional_info  line)
x = run_transformer(embeddings)
options: The model reads the two sentences as essentially the same bag of tokens, because without position it has no sense of word order
The model reads sentence B correctly but reverses sentence A
The model refuses to run and raises an error
The model works perfectly — positional information is optional
challenges.explanation: A transformer sees all tokens at once and has no built-in notion of order. Positional information is what tells it token 1 came before token 2. Skip it, and "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" contain the same tokens and look identical to the model — word order is lost.

challenges.prompt: A learner explains why transformers replaced older RNNs like this: "Transformers are faster because each token only ever looks at the single token right before it, so there's less work to do." What's the correction?
challenges.code:
# The learner's claim:
# "Each token only looks at the ONE token before it,
#  which is why transformers are fast and better than RNNs."
options: Correct — transformers only look at the previous token, which is why they're fast

It's backwards: self-attention lets every token look at ALL other tokens directly, and it's this all-at-once wiring (not looking at fewer tokens) that gives parallelism and better long-range connections
The real reason is that transformers have no feed-forward network, so there's less to compute
Transformers are actually slower than RNNs; the claim has the speed backwards but the mechanism right
challenges.explanation: The claim gets the mechanism exactly backwards. RNNs are the ones that pass information along a chain one step at a time. Self-attention instead lets every token attend directly to every other token in a single step. That direct, all-at-once connectivity is what makes transformers parallelizable on GPUs and far better at linking distant words — not looking at fewer tokens.
